name: Sasangohar, Farzan
recent_1: Wang, Z., Ahmadi, N., Mehta, R. K., & Sasangohar, F. (2026). The Dynamics of Attention Across Automated and Manual Driving Modes: A Driving Simulation Study. Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/00187208261447877
recent_1_synopsis: A driving-simulator study examining how drivers' attention allocation shifts between periods of automated and manual control, characterizing the dynamics of (re)engagement at mode transitions. It matters for designing automation hand-off and driver-monitoring strategies that mitigate out-of-the-loop performance decrements in partially automated vehicles.
recent_2: Ziyadidegan, S., Razavi, M., Pesarakli, H., Javid, A., & Sasangohar, F. (2026). Investigating Occupational Stress in ICU Nurses: A Comparative Analysis of Mental and Physical Stress Using Heart Rate Variability and Wrist-Worn Accelerometer Data. Human Factors, 68(5), 545-562. DOI: 10.1177/00187208261417155
recent_2_synopsis: Using wearable physiological sensing, this study separates mental from physical stress in intensive-care nurses by combining heart-rate-variability metrics with wrist-worn accelerometer activity data. It advances naturalistic, multimodal stress measurement for safety-critical clinical work and informs workload-management and staffing interventions in the ICU.
recent_3: Emond, W., Sauerbier, R., Scholly, U., Sasangohar, F., & Zare, M. (2026). Motion sickness detection and mitigation in a stop-and-go passenger ride scenario. Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour, 116, 103381. DOI: 10.1016/j.trf.2025.103381
recent_3_synopsis: A within-subjects driving study that induced and then alleviated motion sickness during repeated stop-and-go laps, testing a multimodal anti-motion-sickness intervention (visual motion cues, reclined posture, music, fragrance, and ventilation). It contributes detection methods and mitigation strategies relevant to passenger comfort in automated and electric vehicles where occupants are increasingly non-drivers.
seminal_1: Son, C., Hegde, S., Smith, A., Wang, X., & Sasangohar, F. (2020). Effects of COVID-19 on College Students' Mental Health in the United States: Interview Survey Study. Journal of Medical Internet Research, 22(9), e21279. DOI: 10.2196/21279
seminal_1_synopsis: An interview-survey study of 195 students at a large U.S. public university documenting how the COVID-19 pandemic affected student stress, anxiety, depression, and coping. With roughly 4,500+ citations (Google Scholar), it is among the most-cited empirical accounts of pandemic-era college mental health and foundational for subsequent work on student well-being and remote-learning stressors.
seminal_2: Wang, X., Hegde, S., Son, C., Keller, B., Smith, A., & Sasangohar, F. (2020). Investigating Mental Health of US College Students During the COVID-19 Pandemic: Cross-Sectional Survey Study. Journal of Medical Internet Research, 22(9), e22817. DOI: 10.2196/22817
seminal_2_synopsis: A companion cross-sectional survey quantifying depression and anxiety severity among U.S. college students during the early pandemic, complementing the qualitative interview study. With roughly 2,100+ citations (Google Scholar), it is a widely used reference for prevalence estimates of student mental-health symptoms during COVID-19.
expertise_overview: Human factors and human-systems engineering in multi-task, safety-critical environments, with a strong emphasis on healthcare and clinical work systems-ICU nursing workload, occupational stress, fatigue, interruptions, clinician burnout, and patient-safety processes. A defining methodological thread is naturalistic, sensor-based measurement of human state: wearable and continuous monitoring of heart rate, heart-rate variability, electrodermal activity, skin temperature, eye-tracking, and accelerometry to quantify stress, workload, and fatigue in situ. He also studies mental health and behavioral health technologies, including mobile mental-health applications, remote monitoring, telemedicine and telecritical care, and PTSD/stress interventions. A second application area is transportation and process-control safety, spanning driver attention and automation transitions, motion sickness, offshore shiftwork fatigue, procedural work, and resilience and emergency management. Methods range from controlled simulator and laboratory experiments to naturalistic field studies, qualitative interviews and Delphi/critical-decision elicitation, surveys, and machine-learning analysis of physiological signals.
notes: Confidently identified (found = yes). Identity confirmed as Farzan Sasangohar, Associate Professor of Industrial & Systems Engineering and Environmental & Occupational Health at Texas A&M University (matches user note), via the Texas A&M faculty profile (engineering.tamu.edu/industrial/profiles/fsasangohar.html), the Applied Cognitive Ergonomics Lab (acelab.tamu.edu), and his Google Scholar profile (user=pw5eXZv0YCQC). PhD in Industrial/Human Factors Engineering, University of Toronto; SM, MIT. Citation counts are from Google Scholar accessed 2026-06-17: total ~11,007 citations, h-index 34; seminal_1 ~4,578 cites and seminal_2 ~2,101 cites. The two seminal papers are his most-cited works by a wide margin. Recent papers were selected as his three most recent (all 2026): two are pre-verified Human Factors entries and the third (Motion Sickness, Transportation Research Part F vol. 116, art. 103381) was confirmed in Crossref. All five DOIs were verified via the Crossref API (api.crossref.org/works/<DOI>) on today's date 2026-06-17, each returning HTTP 200 with a matching title. No unverifiable citations were used. Author lists for recent_1 and recent_2 were reconstructed from secondary sources and may have minor ordering inaccuracies, but titles, venues, years, and DOIs are Crossref-verified.
